terms.definition: Reversible graph transformation. Same data viewed in a different representation. No information lost. Can always be undone. Primary operation of the data transformation system.
Irreversible transformation. Information is destroyed or genuinely created. New structure emerges that cannot be walked back. Permitted only in the logic rewriting system.

Shared store of rule-invariant pairs discovered through labelled training. Has two layers: empirical (label-correlated, permanent) and reasoned (logic-derived, requires human approval to unlock). Read by both systems, written only by the data system (empirical layer).
A library entry consisting of a subgraph paired with the rule context that produced it during traversal. A subgraph alone has no diagnostic value — the rule context is required.
Layer 1 of the shared library. Contains only label-correlated entries. Permanent — never overwritten. Serves as ground truth anchor for the whole system.
Layer 2 of the shared library. Contains logic-derived entries. Only unlocked after human approval of capability threshold. Can be audited and diffed against empirical library.
Collapsing subgraphs upward — compressing two layers into one. A perspective change, not terrain restructuring. Governed by the data system.
Expanding subgraphs downward — decomposing one layer into two. Governed by the data system.
Recognition is not one test but a cascade of partial matches climbing layers until a threshold is met. E.g. pixel layer: no pattern; edge layer: partial structure; object layer: isomorphism fires.
